schools.summary: Gill: some take this of the moralizing Gentiles (Socrates, Cato, Seneca) who reproved others yet did the same, and so are inexcusable, others of the Jews who despised and condemned the Gentiles; Barnes: the appeal to their own conviction of right; Paul charging every man who judges another while doing the same as inexcusable, self-condemned by his own judgment (the knowledge that aggravates, the conscience the witness) — the universal hypocrisy exposed (the self-righteous judge, the moralist, the Jew), the argument turning from the Gentiles (ch.1) to all who judge, shutting every mouth (Romans 3:9, 19), preparing the way for grace.

themes: thou art inexcusable, O man... that judgest
thou condemnest thyself; for thou doest the same

schools.summary: Gill: 'the judgment of God' here not what is exercised in this life but the judgment to come — certain, universal, carried on by the omniscient and omnipotent God, and definitive; Barnes: 'we are sure' (we know) the common, admitted sentiment of mankind that God will punish such crimes; God's judgment according to truth — the real state of things, the actual guilt, not appearances (the contrast with the partial, hypocritical judgment of men), the righteous judgment (no respecter of persons, v.11, Genesis 18:25), the inescapability for the hypocrite (who escapes men's judgment but not God's true judgment).

themes: the judgment of God is according to truth
not the partial judgment of men

schools.summary: Barnes: the appeal to common sense and the instinctive conviction of right — if they condemned those who practised these things (imperfect and unholy as their own sense of justice was), would not a holy and just God be far more likely to pronounce judgment, and could they escape who had delivered a similar sentence (the argument from the lesser to the greater); Paul exposing the folly of the hypocrite who supposes he will escape the judgment he passes on others — the vain presumption of impunity (the self-deception that condemns others yet excuses self, the hypocrite's own judgment witnessing against him, Luke 19:22).

themes: thinkest thou... that thou shalt escape the judgment of God?

schools.summary: Gill: Paul anticipates the objection from prosperity (that their prospering proved them not so wicked, or that they would escape), but it was not their innocence but the riches of God's goodness, longsuffering, and forbearance that gave them prosperity; Clarke/Barnes: the purpose of the goodness to lead to repentance; the riches of God's goodness (kindness), forbearance (holding back judgment), and longsuffering (patience) meant to lead to repentance (2 Peter 3:9, 15, the patience giving space to turn, the kindness that should melt the heart) — the sin of despising it (abusing the patience as impunity, hardening under it, the mercy abused aggravating the guilt).

themes: the riches of his goodness and forbearance and longsuffering
the goodness of God leadeth thee to repentance

schools.summary: Gill distinguishes natural hardness (in every son of Adam), acquired habitual hardness (increased by sinning), and judicial hardness (which God gives sinners up to for sin); an impenitent heart one that cannot of itself repent; the hard, impenitent heart, abusing God's patience, treasuring up wrath against the day of wrath — the dreadful accumulation of judgment (every unrepented sin adding to the store, as a man stores treasure, the patience abused increasing the wrath), the day of wrath and the revelation of the righteous judgment of God (Revelation 6:17, the just sentence long delayed then made manifest).

Gill: God will render to wicked men according to the demerit of their sins (eternal damnation) and to good men eternal life — not according to the merit of their good works (which have none) but according to their nature, the believer's works the fruit of grace; the judgment 'according to works' not contradicting justification by faith (the works the evidence and fruit of saving faith, not the meritorious ground, the wicked's works showing unbelief, the believer's showing faith, James 2:26) — justification by faith alone but the faith that justifies not alone (it works), the believer's reward itself the gift of grace (Romans 6:23, the grace crowning its own work).

debates.question: Does judgment 'according to works' contradict justification by faith?

sides.position: No — the believer's works are the evidence and fruit of saving faith, not the meritorious ground; the judgment by works displays the reality of faith, the reward itself of grace

themes: render to every man according to his deeds
not according to the merit of works

schools.summary: Gill: these words describe one sort of persons to whom God will render according to their works — not the best-moralized Gentiles (who sought worldly things and applause), nor the pharisaical Jews (who sought righteousness by works and honour from men), nor any unregenerate persons, but only those who have the true principles of grace implanted in them; the persevering well-doer (patient continuance, not fitful or self-seeking, Revelation 2:10) seeking glory, honour, immortality (the eternal not the worldly, Colossians 3:1) — eternal life rendered to the grace-wrought obedience of the regenerate (the fruit of grace, the reward of grace to its own work).

themes: seek for glory and honour and immortality

schools.summary: Gill: 'them that are of the contention' — who contend for victory and not truth, are quarrelsome, and sow discord; Barnes: 'contentious' here denotes a disposition toward God — rebellious, opposing God, contending with the Almighty and resisting his claims; the contentious who do not obey the truth (the gospel, the revealed will) but obey unrighteousness (serve sin) — the rebellious heart that resists God and serves the wrong master (the contrast with the believer who obeys the truth, v.7), the doom of indignation and wrath (the divine displeasure rendered for the disposition, the judgment by the heart's allegiance, the moral ground).

themes: do not obey the truth, but obey unrighteousness

schools.summary: Gill: these words express the second death, the torments of hell, the worm that never dies; Barnes: 'tribulation' denotes affliction, being pressed down by punishment, 'anguish' (a strong word) the extreme distress of the soul under the reward of sin; tribulation and anguish upon every evildoer (the universal doom, no escape for any), 'of the Jew first, and also of the Gentile' — the Jew, having the greater privilege (the law, the oracles), first in accountability (the privilege increasing the responsibility, Luke 12:48, the greater light the greater reckoning), the impartial doom within the order (the same wrath on all evildoers, v.11).

themes: tribulation and anguish, upon every soul... that doeth evil
of the Jew first, and also of the Gentile

schools.summary: Gill: glory, honour, and peace are words for the everlasting happiness of the saints — a crown of glory, an honour exceeding the greatest potentates, a peace that passes all understanding; Clarke: while the impenitent experience indignation, every man that worketh good (that lives in obedience) receives glory and honour; glory, honour, and peace to every well-doer, 'to the Jew first, and also to the Gentile' — the reward of grace-wrought obedience (as v.7, the fruit of grace not the natural man's striving) falling impartially on all who do good (the no-respect-of-persons, v.11, the grace extending to all within the order of privilege).

themes: glory, honour, and peace, to every man that worketh good
to the Jew first, and also to the Gentile

schools.summary: Gill: at the day of judgment it will not come into consideration of what nation men are, from what parents descended, of what age, sex, state, or sect, nor whether they conformed to such externals, but only whether they are righteous men or sinners; God no respecter of persons (the impartiality grounding v.9-10) — judging not by nation, privilege, or outward distinction but by the real character (Acts 10:34, the leveling of Jew and Gentile, the Jew's privilege no exemption, the Gentile's lack no excuse), the warning to the privileged (the advantages no shelter) and the comfort to the Gentile (not excluded, the door open to all).

themes: there is no respect of persons with God
judging by real character, not privilege

schools.summary: Gill: the Gentiles 'without law' (the written law of Moses) yet not without the law of nature in their breasts, sinned against the God of nature and the light of nature, and so shall perish; Barnes: this shows the principle on which God treats men so as not to be a respecter of persons; each judged by the light he had — the Gentile by nature (perishing without the written law, condemned for the light of nature he sinned against), the Jew by the law (judged by the standard he possessed) — the impartial principle (the judgment proportioned to the light, v.11), the just condemnation of both establishing universal guilt (Romans 3:19), the way prepared for the gospel.

themes: sinned without law shall... perish without law
sinned in the law shall be judged by the law
each judged by the light he had

schools.summary: Gill: the Jews justly condemned despite having and hearing the law, since hearing without doing will never make a person righteous before God (the bare hearing every sabbath, without practical obedience, falling short); Barnes: the general principle that the doers of the law only can be justified, if justification is attempted by the law; not hearers but doers justified — the law demands doing (perfect obedience, Galatians 3:10), condemning the Jewish hearer; since none perfectly do it (Romans 3:23) the principle drives all to Christ (Romans 3:20, the law the schoolmaster), a statement of the law's standard not a teaching of justification by works (harmonized with Romans 3-4).

debates.question: Does 'the doers of the law shall be justified' teach justification by works?

sides.position: No — it states the law's standard (if any perfectly did it he would be justified); since none does, it condemns the hearer and drives all to Christ for the righteousness the law cannot give

themes: not the hearers... but the doers of the law shall be justified

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle owns the Gentiles had not the written law of Moses, yet intimates they had a law against which they sinned and which they in part obeyed; Barnes: the same principle (v.13) applies to the heathen; the Gentiles doing by nature the things of the law, a law to themselves (their own conscience and moral sense the rule) — most taking it of pagan Gentiles' natural conscience (some, as Augustine, of Gentile Christians by grace); the witness of natural conscience (the moral knowledge in all men, the law written on the heart, v.15), not a way of salvation (the Gentiles sin against it, Romans 1:21, 32) but a proof of accountability (making them inexcusable, justly judged, v.12).

debates.question: Who are the 'Gentiles which do by nature the things in the law'?

sides.position: Pagan Gentiles, whose natural conscience does some things of the law, establishing their accountability (the chief sense)
Gentile Christians who do the law by grace

themes: Gentiles... do by nature the things contained in the law

schools.summary: Gill: though the Gentiles had not the law in form, written on tables, they had 'the work' (the matter and substance) of it in their minds, as their practices show; the moral law written on Adam's heart at creation, obliterated by the fall, its remnants retained by the Gentiles; the work of the law written on the heart, the conscience bearing witness, the thoughts accusing or excusing (the inward debate, the moral self-judgment) — the inward witness of moral accountability (the universal conscience, the remnant of the original righteousness, Romans 1:20), distinguished from the law written on the heart in regeneration (Jeremiah 31:33, the new covenant).

themes: the work of the law written in their hearts

schools.summary: Gill: this to be read in connection with v.12, expressing the time when both Jews and Gentiles will be judged — a 'day' both for the clearness of the judgment and for the certain (though unknown) time fixed; Barnes: the intermediate verses (13-15) a parenthesis, God judging all in righteousness, the Jew by the law and the heathen by conscience; God judging the secrets of men (the hidden thoughts, motives, deeds, the searching judgment, 1 Chronicles 28:9) by Jesus Christ (Christ the Judge, John 5:22; Acts 17:31) according to Paul's gospel (the judgment by Christ a part of the gospel message), the day gathering the whole argument.

themes: God shall judge the secrets of men
the searching judgment of the hidden things

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul sets down the glories of the Jews (owing to which they scorned the Gentiles) — first the name, of great majesty (as the name Christian is now), yet Paul does not say 'thou art a Jew' but 'art called' so, 'and makest thy boast in God' (as being loved and honoured above all men), gently mocking the boast (the name without the reality); the Jew resting in the law (relying on the having and hearing of it, the false rest, not the doing, v.13) and boasting in God (the confidence in the privilege) — the privileges rehearsed (v.17-20) only to be turned against him (v.21-24, the privilege abused condemning).

themes: restest in the law... boast of God

schools.summary: Gill: 'his will' not the secret will nor the gospel (of which they were ignorant) but his declared will in the law (showing what is and is not to be done), to know this a privilege other peoples had not, but the bare knowledge of no avail (persons may know their Lord's will and not do it, Luke 12:47); Barnes: this knowledge came from the Scriptures; the Jew knowing God's will and approving the excellent things, instructed by the law (the real privilege, Romans 3:2) — yet a knowledge that condemns if not obeyed (James 4:17, the light aggravating the disobedience, the greater light the greater guilt, the privilege a peril if abused).

themes: knowest his will, and approvest the excellent

schools.summary: Barnes: 'art confident' denotes the full assurance of the Jew that he was superior in knowledge to all others — a remarkable fact that the Jews put the fullest confidence in their religion (though proud and hypocritical, not speculative infidels but fully assured God was the Author of their institutions); the Jew confident as a guide of the blind (the Gentiles, ignorant of God) and a light to those in darkness — the lofty self-estimation as the teacher of the nations (the pride of the privileged, the self-appointed instructor), the truth in the privilege (the real advantage of the oracles) abused without obedience, soon exposed by his failure to teach himself (v.21).

themes: art confident... a guide of the blind
a light of them which are in darkness

schools.summary: Gill: 'an instructor of the foolish' the common people among the Jews who knew not the law (the Jewish doctors and masters called 'an instructor'); Barnes: 'the foolish' here means those void of understanding whom the Jew esteemed himself qualified to teach, 'babes' figurative; the Jew claiming to instruct the ignorant and teach the simple, having 'the form of knowledge and of the truth in the law' — the outward form and framework of knowledge (the shape of truth without the power, 2 Timothy 3:5, the letter without the spirit, v.29, the knowledge without the practice), setting up the indictment (v.21-24, the teacher who does not do).

themes: an instructor of the foolish, a teacher of babes
the form of knowledge and of the truth
the shape of truth without the power

schools.summary: Gill: though put as questions, these are to be taken as assertions and matters of fact — the Jewish teachers, though they taught others, did not teach themselves (blind leaders of the blind, ignorant of the spirituality of the law); Paul turning the boast against the Jew (he who teaches another does not teach himself, he who preaches against stealing steals) — the hypocrisy of the teacher who does not practice what he preaches (the gap between profession and practice, the self-condemnation, v.1, Luke 4:23), the indictment grounded in fact (the actual hypocrisy), the dishonour to the teaching (the law reproached by his breaking it).

themes: thou which teachest another, teachest thou not thyself?
preachest... not steal, dost thou steal?

schools.summary: Gill: the adultery here to be taken literally, not figuratively; Barnes: no doubt this was a crime common among the Jews (the Talmud accusing some of their celebrated rabbis), and that abhorring idols they yet committed sacrilege (robbing temples, profaning sacred things, or robbing God in tithes, Malachi 3:8); the Jew forbidding adultery yet committing it, abhorring idols yet committing sacrilege — the further exposure of the gap between profession and practice (the hypocrisy in the very sins condemned, the boast belied by the deeds), the depth of the hypocrisy reaching the gravest sins, the partial/selective conscience (abhorring one sin while committing a kindred one).

themes: sayest... not commit adultery, dost thou commit adultery?
abhorrest idols, dost thou commit sacrilege?
the boast belied by the deeds

schools.summary: Gill: the Jew boasted of having the law, of his knowledge of it, and even of keeping it to perfection; Barnes: to boast in the law implied conviction of its excellence and obligation (one does not boast of what he esteems worthless), by boasting they proclaimed it was from God and by breaking it they denied it — and since actions are the true test of men's real opinions, their breaking the law dishonoured God more than their boasting honoured him; the contradiction of the boast and the breach (the law professed but transgressed, dishonouring its Author, the deeds the louder witness, the dishonour exceeding the honour).

themes: makest thy boast of the law
through breaking the law dishonourest thou God?

schools.summary: Gill: the being and perfections of God, and his law and worship, denied or evil spoken of; Barnes: the Jews' conduct led the heathen world to blaspheme and reproach both their religion and its Author — by their hypocrisy and crimes the pagan world led to despise a religion observed to have no effect in purifying its professors, the reproach terminating on its Author; the name of God blasphemed among the Gentiles through the Jews' sin (the dreadful effect of the professing people's inconsistent life, the dishonour to God, 2 Peter 2:2), grounded in Scripture ('as it is written,' Isaiah 52:5; Ezekiel 36:20-23) — the solemn warning to honour the holy name by the holy life.

themes: the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles through you

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle allows circumcision was profitable (while it was in force, before its abrogation), but only in case the law was kept; Barnes: circumcision the rite by which the relation to the covenant of Abraham was recognised; circumcision profiting if the law is kept, but to the law-breaker becoming as uncircumcision (the outward sign worthless without the obedience it signified, the form without the power, v.20, the sign without the thing signified — circumcision pointing to the circumcision of the heart, v.29, void without the reality), the privilege voided by disobedience, the principle applied to all outward signs and privileges (the sacrament without the grace worthless).

themes: circumcision profiteth, if thou keep the law
a breaker of the law... made uncircumcision
the outward worthless without the inward

schools.summary: Gill: the words spoken hypothetically (if any such Gentile could be found who keeps the law), absolutely understood only of those who from a principle of grace act in obedience, in the hands of Christ, looking to him as the fulfilling end of the law for righteousness; Barnes: the heathen who keeps the righteousness of the law; the uncircumcised who keeps the law's righteousness counted for circumcision — the reality (the obedient, believing heart) accepted without the sign where the sign without the reality is rejected (v.25, the substance over the form, the heart over the rite, v.29), the believing Gentile (by grace, in Christ) reckoned the true circumcised, levelling the Jewish privilege (v.11).

debates.question: Who is the 'uncircumcision' that keeps the law's righteousness?

sides.position: Spoken hypothetically, but absolutely true only of the believing Gentile who by grace, in Christ, keeps the righteousness of the law

themes: if the uncircumcision keep the righteousness of the law

schools.summary: Gill: the Gentiles by nature uncircumcised (circumcision by command and performed by art, uncircumcision by nature, what men naturally have); Barnes: if the uncircumcised keep the law, their conduct condemns the Jew (as a more consistent and upright man condemns us by his example); the naturally uncircumcised who fulfils the law judging the Jew who, with 'the letter and circumcision' (the written law and the sign), transgresses it — the obedient Gentile a standing rebuke to the privileged law-breaker (the reality condemning the empty form, the condemnation by example, Matthew 12:41), overturning the Jewish confidence (the privilege no defense, the boast overturned).

themes: uncircumcision by nature... if it fulfil the law, judge thee
by the letter and circumcision dost transgress
the obedient Gentile judging the Jew

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle removes the plea from name and privilege by distinguishing between a Jew and a Jew, and between circumcision and circumcision — he is not a Jew who is one outwardly (by mere name, nature, nation, religion, and profession); Barnes: he who is merely descended from Abraham, circumcised, and externally conforming does not possess the true character contemplated by the separation of the Jewish people (which required much more); the merely outward Jew and outward circumcision not the true (the outward profession not the ground of acceptance, Romans 9:6, the true Jew defined by reality not name and rite), the principle reaching all outward religion.

themes: he is not a Jew, which is one outwardly
neither is that circumcision... outward in the flesh
the true Jew defined by reality

schools.summary: Gill: the true Jew has an internal work of grace upon his soul — not only an outward name but an inward nature, not the law in the hand but in the heart, not external righteousness only but internal holiness, a worshipper of God in Spirit and in truth; Barnes: such a one comes up to the design of the Jewish institution, having the true spirit; the true Jew one inwardly, the circumcision of the heart, in the spirit not the letter (the inward, spiritual reality the outward sign foreshadowed, Deuteronomy 10:16; 30:6, the heart's renewal by the Spirit), whose praise is of God not men (who sees the heart; a play on 'Judah/Jew' meaning praise) — the true people of God marked by the renewed heart.

themes: he is a Jew, which is one inwardly
circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit
